cards.text: Malaysia's RM31.3B 1MDB recovery headline includes a Goldman guarantee Goldman is fighting to reduce
cards.sub: Of Goldman's USD3.9B 2020 settlement, USD2.5B was cash. The remaining USD1.4B (about RM6B) is an asset-recovery guarantee Goldman filed at the LCIA on 11 October 2023 to contest. The arbitration is ongoing.

cards.h: Of Goldman's USD3.9B 2020 settlement, only USD2.5B was paid in cash
cards.s: Goldman filed at the London Court of International Arbitration on 11 October 2023, arguing Malaysia had already recovered enough from third parties to satisfy the USD1.4B guarantee. MOF told Parliament in October 2025 that the dispute remained under arbitration.
cards.d: Under the 24 July 2020 settlement, Goldman agreed to pay Malaysia USD2.5 billion cash plus a guarantee that Malaysia would receive at least USD1.4 billion in proceeds from related asset seizures. On 11 October 2023 Goldman filed at the LCIA, arguing Malaysia had already recovered enough from third parties to satisfy that guarantee; Malaysia's position is that Goldman is trying to avoid an interim payment obligation of USD250 million. MOF's 15 October 2025 written parliamentary reply confirmed the arbitration was being handled by Malaysia's legal team and details were confidential. The MACC's May 2026 RM31.3 billion cumulative figure includes the full Goldman USD3.9 billion settlement; whether and how the contested USD1.4 billion tranche is treated as 'recovered' inside that total has not been itemised in any public document.

cards.h: Of cumulative 1MDB recoveries, RM5.29B remains in the trust account against RM24.46B still owed
cards.s: MOF told Parliament in November 2025 that of RM31.2B recovered into the Asset Recovery Trust Account, only RM5.29B remained — the rest had been drawn down to service 1MDB debt. Outstanding 1MDB obligations stand at RM24.46B including shareholder advances.
cards.d: Cumulative recovery and net fiscal position are two different numbers. MOF's 27 November 2025 parliamentary reply showed RM31.2 billion linked to 1MDB and SRC had been recovered into the Asset Recovery Trust Account, of which RM5.29 billion remained, while RM24.46 billion in 1MDB obligations remained outstanding including IMTN principal/interest and shareholder advances. An earlier 14 August 2025 MOF reply set the picture: RM42.17 billion in net fund had been channelled to 1MDB up to 31 July 2025, with RM15.44 billion sourced from MOF/MOF Inc funds and RM26.73 billion from recovered 1MDB assets; outstanding IMTN debt was RM9.02 billion. The headline tells you what came in. The parliamentary replies tell you how much went straight back out to service the same debt, how much of the inflow was direct taxpayer money rather than recovery, and how much is still owed.

cards.h: MACC's '74.5% recovered' uses 1MDB's 2015 debt as the denominator; the DOJ's misappropriation baseline is alleged at US$4.5 billion
cards.s: RM42B is 1MDB's accumulated 2015 debt. The DOJ, in its June 2024 release, alleged in civil forfeiture complaints that 'more than US$4.5 billion' (about RM21B at 2024 rates) was diverted between 2009 and 2015. Different denominator, different sentence.
cards.d: The RM42 billion figure has hardened into a Malaysian press standard, but its origin is 1MDB's total debt at the point of the 2015 ratings collapse, which includes legitimately-issued bonds that funded real (if mismanaged) projects. The US Department of Justice, in its 13 June 2024 release confirming USD1.4 billion total repatriation under the Kleptocracy Asset Recovery Initiative, used a different baseline: alleged in civil forfeiture complaints that 'more than US$4.5 billion in funds' was misappropriated by senior officials and associates between 2009 and 2015. The two are not interchangeable. The RM31.3 billion recovery numerator is itself a mix: it combines returned principal, bank settlements, punitive penalties (Goldman's settlement alone exceeds the cash misappropriated through it), and asset seizures. A 74.5% figure that mixes a total-debt denominator with a mixed-composition numerator and benchmarks against an alleged misappropriation baseline gives readers a confident percentage that papers over three separate accounting choices.

cards.text: The question is not how much was recovered. It is which number the next press release counts.

cards.h: Think of the headline like a sales total that includes a customer disputing the charge
cards.text: Malaysia put the full RM31.3B on the receipt. RM6B of it is a Goldman guarantee still in arbitration. Most of what cleared the trust account has already been paid out to service old 1MDB debt.

RM31.3 billion in cumulative assets recovered is a major outcome and one of the largest financial-crime claw-backs on record. But the framing of '74.5% of a settled scandal' does work the underlying ledger cannot yet bear. The headline counts a Goldman tranche still in arbitration, it uses 2015 total debt rather than the DOJ's alleged-misappropriation baseline as the denominator, it mixes returned principal with bank penalties in the numerator, and it obscures that most of what cleared the trust account has already been drawn down to service 1MDB debt — with RM24.46 billion still owed. The honest reading is narrower: a substantial share of what was alleged to have been misappropriated has been clawed back, the headline figure is partly conditional on an arbitration not yet decided, and the net fiscal position remains in deficit. These figures will be cited by every administration that holds them. Holding them straight is the work.

headline: RM31.3B Recovered, RM24.46B Still Owed, RM6B Still In Arbitration
context: MACC announced in May 2026 that 1MDB cumulative recovery had reached RM31.3 billion, or 74.5% of the RM42 billion described as misappropriated. MOF parliamentary replies in August and November 2025 show only RM5.29 billion remains in the asset-recovery trust account, and RM24.46 billion in 1MDB obligations is still outstanding. Goldman's USD1.4 billion settlement guarantee remains contested in London arbitration.
sources: MOF press portal (Ahmad Maslan 13 Mar 2023; written replies 14 Aug 2025, 15 Oct 2025, 27 Nov 2025); Bernama (MACC press conference 6 May 2026); Malay Mail; The Star; FMT; The Edge Malaysia; Business Today; US DOJ press releases (13 Jun 2024; 17 Jan 2025); US SEC Administrative Proceeding 3-20131 (22 Oct 2020); Goldman Sachs press release 24 Jul 2020; Bloomberg; Reuters; SCMP; Al Jazeera; New Straits Times (Johari Feb 2025); Channel News Asia (Anwar parliamentary statement); Transparency International Malaysia.
confidence: High — Goldman settlement structure and LCIA arbitration directly verified against SEC 3-20131, Goldman's own 2020 release, and MOF's 15 October 2025 parliamentary reply. Updated trust-account and outstanding-obligation figures (RM31.2B / RM5.29B / RM24.46B) verified against MOF's November 2025 parliamentary reply via Bernama. MACC's RM31.3 billion total and 74.5% headline verified against Bernama's report of the 6 May 2026 press conference; whether the contested USD1.4 billion tranche is itemised inside the total has not been disclosed and is acknowledged as such. DOJ baseline framed with DOJ's own legal qualifier ('as alleged in civil forfeiture complaints').
revisions_made: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Hook claim 'if Goldman wins, the headline drops overnight' removed as unverified speculation; reframed to verifiable inclusion claim.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): LCIA filing date precision — 11 October 2023 (from MOF parliamentary reply), not just 'October 2023'.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'As of February 2025... most recent public update' updated to October 2025 MOF parliamentary reply, which is more recent.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Economic fact card fully rewritten using August 2025 + November 2025 MOF parliamentary replies. Stale RM43.8B/RM9.7B March 2023 figures replaced with RM31.2B trust / RM5.29B remaining / RM24.46B outstanding / RM42.17B net fund channelled / RM26.73B from recoveries / RM15.44B from MOF.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Removed simple-subtraction 'RM12.5B in the hole' framing — flagged as misleading because debt servicing was partly funded by recovered assets.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'DOJ established' replaced with 'DOJ, in its June 2024 release, alleged in civil forfeiture complaints' per DOJ's own legal language.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'proven misappropriation has been more than fully recovered' framing dropped — flagged as misleading because the recovery numerator is mixed (principal + settlements + penalties) and the DOJ baseline is alleged not proven.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'RM31.3 billion is real money returned to Malaysia' softened to 'RM31.3 billion in cumulative assets recovered' — MACC reported total value of assets recovered, not necessarily cash received.
REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias): Governance card now explicitly names that the numerator is a mix of returned principal, bank settlements, and punitive penalties — addressing the false-equivalence flag.

REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias): Governance card acknowledges that the RM42B baseline is a legitimate fiscal accounting choice (the state is liable for the full debt regardless of misappropriation), while still noting that the public hears '74.5% of stolen money' when the underlying figures describe '74.5% of total debt against a mixed-numerator recovery'.

ADDED (Stage 4): Stage 4 noted Finance Minister II Amir Hamzah disclosed RM48.06B total 1MDB-related payments as of March 2024 — referenced contextually in the broader trust-account picture; not used as the headline figure because the August/November 2025 MOF replies are more recent and more itemised.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): View card now explicitly names that 'these figures will be cited by every administration that holds them' — cross-party narrative-management point. Racial-framing version of this point ('Malay political elite') from Stage 5 explicitly dropped per the 3R rule; the structural point about elite-vs-rakyat cost distribution is preserved without race-keyed wording.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'Most of what cleared the trust account has already been drawn down to service old 1MDB debt' is arithmetic from MOF November 2025 figures (RM31.2B recovered minus RM5.29B remaining = RM25.91B drawn down). Self-verified from primary source.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'Goldman's settlement alone exceeds the cash misappropriated through it' — verified against DOJ June 2024 figure (USD600M cash bribes/kickbacks through Goldman bond transactions; settlement USD3.9B). Used as illustration of mixed-numerator point.

introduced_claims.claim: Most of what cleared the trust account has already been drawn down to service old 1MDB debt
introduced_claims.primary_source: MOF parliamentary reply 27 Nov 2025 via Bernama: RM31.2B recovered into trust, RM5.29B remaining; arithmetic RM31.2B - RM5.29B = RM25.91B drawn down.

introduced_claims.claim: Goldman's settlement alone exceeds the cash misappropriated through it
introduced_claims.primary_source: DOJ 13 Jun 2024 release on KARI repatriation, which describes the Goldman bond transactions and the bribes paid; Goldman 24 Jul 2020 settlement of USD3.9B exceeds the bribery-flow estimates.

synthesis_notes: Three over-integration decisions made: (1) Stage 2 said the DOJ-vs-debt denominator dismissal was unfair because the state is liable for the full debt; Stage 3 said the DOJ-baseline comparison was misleading because the numerator is mixed. Both are right and they reinforce — not cancel — each other. The governance card was rewritten to keep the denominator-distinction point (Stage 1 + Stage 3) AND acknowledge fiscal-legitimacy of RM42B (Stage 2) AND name the mixed-composition numerator (Stage 2 + Stage 3). No averaging — additive integration. (2) Stage 5 wanted explicit political-class attribution naming UMNO/Najib by name and 'Malay political elite' framing. Per CLAUDE.md 3R rule (critique policy, not communities), the racial-framing version is dropped. The cross-administration structural point — 'figures cited by every administration that holds them' — is preserved because it implicates the political class as a class without race-keying. (3) Stage 4 wanted B40/gender/East Malaysia perspectives integrated. Given the bite-size budget and the single-issue focus on accounting integrity, only the cross-administration framing made it in. The B40 opportunity-cost point is left for a future follow-up issue rather than padded into this one. Compression decisions: Economic fact card preserved the qualifier 'including shareholder advances' on the RM24.46B figure — this qualifier is load-bearing because the IMTN-only outstanding is RM9.02B; dropping it would understate the liability.
